name: Latvia
excerpt: Several eastern Baltic tribes merged in medieval times to form the ethnic core of the Latvian people (ca. 8th-12th centuries A.D.). The region subsequently came under the control of Germans, Poles, Swedes, and finally Russians.

quick_facts.religions: Lutheran 36.2%, Roman Catholic 19.5%, Orthodox 19.1%, other Christian 1.6%, other 0.1%, unspecified/none 23.5% (2017 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: Latvian 62.7%, Russian 24.5%, Belarusian 3.1%, Ukrainian 2.2%, Polish 2%, Lithuanian 1.1%, other 1.8%, unspecified 2.6% (2021 est.)
quick_facts.natural_resources: peat, limestone, dolomite, amber, hydropower, timber, arable land
quick_facts.major_exports: wood, wheat, broadcasting equipment, packaged medicine, natural gas (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: most of the country is composed of fertile low-lying plains with some hills in the east
summaries.history_summary: Several eastern Baltic tribes merged in medieval times to form the ethnic core of the Latvian people (ca. 8th-12th centuries A.D.). The region subsequently came under the control of Germans, Poles, Swedes, and finally Russians. A Latvian republic emerged following World War I, but the USSR annexed it in 1940 -- an action never recognized by the US and many other countries. Latvia reestablished its independence in 1991 after the breakup of the Soviet Union. Although the last Russian troops left in 1994, the status of the Russian minority (some 25% of the population) remains of concern to Moscow. Latvia joined both NATO and the EU in 2004; it joined the euro zone in 2014 and the OECD in 2016.
summaries.government_summary: parliamentary republic. Independence: 18 November 1918 (from Soviet Russia); 4 May 1990 (declared from the Soviet Union); 6 September 1991 (recognized by the Soviet Union)
summaries.economy_summary: high-income EU and eurozone member; weak recovery following economic contraction, with slight increase in private consumption and uncertain trade environment; challenges from skilled-labor shortages, capital market access, large informal sector, and green and digital transitions

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Latvia's religious identity is split across Lutheran, Catholic, and Orthodox traditions, with nearly a quarter claiming no religion — reflecting weak inherited faith of any kind after Soviet-era atheism. Pray for the clear gospel of salvation by grace through faith alone to break through.
prayer_points: Pray for laborers to plant independent, Bible-believing local churches across a country with very little evangelical presence.
Pray for nominal Lutherans, Catholics, and Orthodox believers alike to encounter genuine, personal faith in Christ.
Pray for Latvian pastors to be trained in sound, Scripture-centered teaching.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Latvia's fragmented, largely nominal religious identity leaves real opportunity for the clear gospel. Ongoing needs include planting autonomous local churches and training national pastors in sound biblical doctrine.
